In December 1998, the Chicago District Office of the EEOC brought this suit against RK Associates in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois. The only document publicly available regarding this case is the docket; therefore, the exact allegations involved are unknown. After a few status conferences, the parties settled in August 1999 through a consent decree.

On December 6, 2023, Jane Doe, a transgender woman, filed a complaint in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia against the Georgia Department of Corrections (“GDC”) for being denied life-saving gender affirming surgeries. The complaint was also filed against GDC’s medical services contractors, including Wellpath and MHM Services, as well as various GDC actors including the commissioner, medical director, and mental health director. The plaintiff was represented by private counsel as well as a non-profit legal organization working to challenge the cruel and inhumane conditions of incarcerated individuals. The case was assigned to Judge Michael L. Brown. 

The complaint alleged that GDC’s blanket ban on providing gender-affirming surgeries, regardless of medical need, to those in their custody was a violation of the Eighth Amendment. Specifically, the complaint argued that a party that had knowledge of the need for medical care in prison and intentionally refused to provide that care was liable for deliberate indifference. A number of Eighth Amendment claims regarding Doe’s solitary confinement and failure to protect Doe from substantial risk by retaining her in a men’s facility were also included. Beyond the Eighth Amendment, the complaint also took issue with the denial of medical services as a failure to provide reasonable accommodations to a qualified individual with disabilities under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA). Doe suffered multiple mental impairments, including gender dysphoria, anxiety, and depression. And finally, the complaint alleged that the Rehabilitation Act, which prohibits discrimination on the basis of disability in any program or activity receiving federal assistance, requires programs to provide “reasonable accommodations” to individuals with disabilities. The denial of medical services thus was a violation of the Rehabilitation Act according to the complaint. Jane Doe ultimately sought injunctive relief. 

The same day the complaint was filed, Doe filed a motion for a preliminary injunction and an expedited hearing on the matter, which was subsequently granted by the court that day. The motion for preliminary injunction requested that the defendants immediately provide her (1) an evaluation for gender-affirming surgery, (2) adequate hormone replacement therapy, (3) gender-affirming commissary items, and (4) a transfer to a women’s prison. Although the preliminary injunction hearing was originally set on December 11, 2023, it was moved to February 12, 2024 due to delays in service of process. 

The case garnered the attention of numerous organizations. On January 8, 2024, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a statement of interest with the court to express its interest in supporting the proper and uniform application of the ADA as it relates to gender dysphoria. The ACLU of Georgia also filed an amicus brief in support of the plaintiff that same day. 

On January 9, 2024, Doe filed a motion to render interim relief in advance of the preliminary injunction hearing; however, the court denied the motion two days later without prejudice, stating that the plaintiff may refile her motion as one for a temporary restraining order or for separate injunctive relief from the prior preliminary injunction motion. 

Following the defendants’ response to the motion for preliminary injunction, a hearing was held over February 12-13. Jane Doe filed an amended complaint on February 13, amending only the names of defendants.  

After the parties admitted numerous exhibits and evidence over the course of the next few months, the court granted in part the plaintiff’s request for gender-affirming commissary items but denied the other requests on April 17. However, the court did order the defendants to provide status updates on the plaintiff’s surgical consultation. See 730 F. Supp. 3d 1327. 

Defendants appealed the preliminary injunction order to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit on April 25 (docket no: 24-11382). The plaintiff filed a motion to dismiss the appeal for lack of appellate jurisdiction on May 23. 

At the appellate level, the parties filed their respective briefs over the course of the summer. On September 12, the plaintiff’s motion to dismiss was carried with the case, to be decided upon by the panel of judges to whom this case was submitted for a decision on the merits. On September 17, an amicus brief was submitted by a host of legal services and civil rights organizations, including the ACLU of Georgia, Lambda Legal Defense and Education Fund, Southern Poverty Law Center, and the Transgender Law Center and Transinclusive Group. Oral arguments were held on December 12. The appellate case remains ongoing. 

At the district court level, the defendants continued to file answers to the plaintiff’s amended complaint and motions to dismiss the complaint over the course of the next few months. On July 15, 2024, the plaintiff filed a motion to extend the April 17 preliminary injunction, which was granted by the court that same day. The preliminary injunction was extended until October 20. Defendants were also ordered to provide status updates about the plaintiff’s surgical consultations every month. 

The district court case then continued on to discovery. On November 15, defendant Wellpath filed a notice of bankruptcy with the district court. As such, the district stayed the motion as to this particular defendant. Plaintiff then filed a temporary restraining order on December 17, compelling defendants to issue a final decision regarding her gender-affirming surgery by a certain date. The court determined that it could not act on the motion for a TRO while the case was stayed as to Wellpath. Accordingly, Doe was told to file a new amended complaint removing Wellpath as a party. As such, the plaintiff filed a second amended complaint removing Wellpath.

Defendants filed a motion to dismiss the second amended complaint in the district court on February 4, 2025. The defendants argued that the plaintiff failed to plausibly allege any Eighth Amendment violations or denial of benefits under the ADA. The motion further claimed that at minimum, all the
personal-capacity claims should be dismissed because of qualified immunity. Due to the stay of the case from Wellpath's bankruptcy, the defendants noted that a decision on this motion could wait until the stay was lifted. The district court case, thus, also remains ongoing as of February 2025.

In 2:00-cv-4497, the EEOC's Newark and Philadelphia offices filed lawsuit against General Motors Corporation (GM) on September 14, 2000 in the District of New Jersey. In 2:00-cv-4735, the EEOC also filed lawsuit against GM in the District of New Jersey, this time on September 28, 2000. A private party complainant, Mary Scott, successfully intervened in 00-4497 in December of 2000. The cases were consolidated for discovery purposes on December 12, 2000. In 00-4735, GM filed a motion to dismiss the case's class action components, which the court subsequently denied. The cases were consolidated in full on February 15, 2001, after which GM again (unsuccessfully) moved to dismiss the class action component of the case. The cases concluded with a consent decree in September 2008. Only the dockets were available in these cases.

In October 2002, the EEOC district office in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania brought this action against O'Brien-Kreitzberg, Inc., a general contracting firm and a subsidiary of the URS Corporation, in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania. The complaint alleged that the defendant violated the Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967 (ADEA) when it refused to hire a job applicant after an interviewer discovered his age. After the defendant filed its initial answer and while a defense motion for summary judgment was pending, the parties settled and a consent decree was entered in November 2003.

In the consent decree, the parties agreed that the defendant would pay the aggrieved party $50,000, refrain from retaliating and discriminating on the basis of age, and post a notice of the suit and stating its commitment to complying with the ADEA. The terms of the decree ran for one year.

The Department of Justice filed this suit against the State of Utah on November 22, 2011, in the U.S. District Court for the District of Utah, Central Division, challenging Utah's recently passed immigration bill, HB 497. It claimed that Sections 3, 10 and 11 of the law were preempted under the Supremacy Clause as an intrusion into the exclusively federal sphere of immigration, and preempted specifically by the Immigration and Nationality Act (INA), 8 U.S.C. §§ 1101 et seq., and sought declaratory and injunctive relief.

This case was immediately consolidated on November 28, 2011 with a challenge brought earlier in the month by private plaintiffs, Utah Coalition of La Raza v. Herbert. See the entry on that case for all subsequent developments.

This case is about an alleged violation of equal representation in Congress.  

On January 11, 2019, a non-profit organization called “Equal Vote America Corp.” (EVA) and an individual filed a complaint in federal court in the Southern District of New York.  The complaint explained that EVA represented a group of eligible voters and that its mission was educating the public on voting rights and promoting voter participation in the election process.  The individual plaintiff was the leader of the organization and also a registered voter in New York.  Plaintiffs filed the complaint against all successive classes of Congress since 1911 and named four congressional leaders because they had the power to initiate new legislation and control the legislative process:  Speaker of the House of Representatives (the House), Minority Leaders in the House and Senate, and Majority Leader in the Senate.  The plaintiffs asked the court to find the statutory method of apportioning House seats under the Apportionment Acts of 1911, 1929, and 1941 (which capped the number of seats at 435 and made the apportionment process self-executing after each 10-year census) to be unconstitutional.  In addition to finding the current law unconstitutional, plaintiffs asked the court to hold Congress in contempt if it did not establish a more equitable method of apportionment. 

The complaint stated that Congress had collectively failed to update the cap and allocation of seats since 1929 in accordance with the “Great Compromise” of a bicameral legislature approach agreed upon by the founding fathers and the Constitutional provisions mandating that House seats be allocated proportional to each state’s population.  Plaintiffs claimed that the significant under-representation in the House had violated the principle of “no taxation without representation” plus their (1) right to equal representation under the Constitution Article I, § 2; (2) right to vote in presidential elections guaranteed by Article II, § 1; (3) privileges guaranteed by Article IV, § 2; (4) Freedom of Speech guaranteed by the First Amendment;  (5) Due Process guaranteed by the Fifth Amendment; (6) Due Process and Equal Protection rights under the Fourteenth Amendment § 1; and (7) right to equal vote under the Fourteenth Amendment § 2.  

The complaint explained that based on the current law and the 2010 Census, Wyoming is guaranteed one seat for its population of 563,626, while New York State gets only 27 for its population of 19,378,561, i.e. 717,707 per house seat. If New York had been allocated one seat per 563,626, same as Wyoming, New York should have had 34.4 seats. Therefore, per the complaint, New York residents had been severely under-represented by at least seven seats, or 21%, which was a direct result of the Apportionment Acts.  Using 1990, 2000, and 2010 census data and mathematical equations, plaintiffs offered what they claimed to be more appropriate state-by-state representation.  Plaintiffs claimed that the requested relief was needed to restore “Every American’s right to equal representation … and equal vote in any election.”  

After several failed attempts, the clerk of the court accepted plaintiffs' first amended complaint on February 11, 2019.  Subsequently, on June 7, 2019, defendants moved to dismiss the amended complaint due to lack of subject matter jurisdiction because the Supreme Court has already upheld Congress’s chosen method of apportionment against constitutional attack. Defendants also moved to dismiss for lack of standing due to their failure to allege any injury that distinguished them from the vast majority of citizens and a failure to allege that any possible alleged harm was caused by the individual defendants’ actions, and because the relief sought would not address the harms alleged.  The defendants also alleged that plaintiffs’ claims were barred by sovereign immunity and absolute immunity. The defendants also alleged that the court lacked personal jurisdiction over three of the four individual defendants because the plaintiffs did not allege sufficient contacts by those individuals with the Southern District of New York.  

On July 7, 2019, the plaintiffs moved to amend their complaint to add 223 individuals from 40 states who joined the lawsuit as co-plaintiffs by signing an online affidavit. 

On September 3, 2019, the trial court judge granted defendants’ motion to dismiss and denied plaintiffs’ motion for leave to amend to add new plaintiffs.  The judge found that plaintiffs had not suffered an injury in fact that conferred standing to challenge the Apportionment Acts, and that EVA had no protectable interest at all because it does not vote and was not entitled to any representation because the right to vote is a citizen’s right to vote.  EVA also failed to qualify for associational standing because it failed to identify by name at least one member with standing.  The judge went on to explain that even if plaintiffs had passed that threshold, the courts routinely rejected claims based on generalized voter standing because the alleged injuries are not distinct and concrete and are shared with the general voting population.  The judge also found that plaintiffs failed to establish a causal nexus between their injuries and defendants’ conduct and failed to establish the court’s ability to redress the plaintiff’s alleged injuries.  The judge explained that because of the separation of powers, the judiciary could not force the legislature to enact new apportionment laws.  In addition, defendants had sovereign and absolute immunity that had not been waived and therefore barred this suit.  The judge agreed with defendants that the addition of new plaintiffs was futile and denied with prejudice the plaintiffs’ motion for leave to amend its complaint.  

On September 23, 2019, plaintiffs appealed.  On October 28, 2020, the 2nd Circuit issued a summary order without precedential effect.  The court affirmed the dismissal but remanded the case to the lower court to amend the judgment dismissing the individual plaintiff’s complaint without prejudice.  The appellate court agreed with the lower court's decision on lack of standing because there was no legally protected interest that was concrete and particularized; because there was no traceable, direct causation; and because the proposed remedies would not provide the requested relief.  The dismissal for lack of standing, however, cannot be dismissed with prejudice—it must be dismissed without prejudice.  When the lower court took no action, the 2nd Circuit issued a mandate to the lower court. Despite the order and mandate, the lower court never amended its judgment to allow the plaintiff to amend his complaint.

Four lawsuits were filed in the District Court for the Northern District of Florida challenging 2021 Florida Senate Bill 90 (SB 90), an omnibus bill that significantly restricted vote-by-mail in the state. The bill also restricted voter registration activity by third-party organizations. Harriet Tubman Freedom Fighters was the fourth lawsuit filed, following League of Women Voters of Florida Inc. v. Lee, Florida State Conference of Branches and Youth Units of the NAACP v. Lee, and Florida Rising Together v. Lee.

On June 14, 2021 Harriett Tubman Freedom Fighters and Head Count, represented by the Southern Poverty Law Center, brought a lawsuit against the Secretary of State and the Florida Attorney General. The Amended Complaint, filed one day later, added additional plaintiffs, Paralyzed Veterans of America and two individuals, and defendants County Supervisors of Elections. The lawsuit challenged SB 90 by alleging that the new voter rules unconstitutionally placed restrictions on organizations that help voters to register, cast their ballots, and participate in the democratic process. The complaint further alleged that SB 90 particularly harmed people with disabilities because it restricted who may assist voters in requesting, receiving, and submitting mail assistance. In this case, Plaintiffs claimed that SB 90 violated the Fourteenth Amendment as void for vagueness, the First Amendment of the US Constitution for compelled speech and infringement on free speech and association, and the Voting Rights Act for limiting assistance to voters with disabilities. Plaintiffs requested declaratory and injunctive relief.

On June 17, 2021, Chief Judge Walker granted the Defendant Secretary of State’s unopposed motion to consolidate for discovery purposes.

The Secretary of State moved to dismiss the four consolidated cases for failure to state a claim on July 30; the Attorney General separately filed a motion to dismiss on August 19. The Court granted in part and denied in part the motions to dismiss on October 8, 2021. Chief Judge Walker dismissed the Voting Rights Act claim for lack of standing because plaintiffs did not allege that the injuries are traceable to Defendants. The Court declined to dismiss all other counts.

On December 8, Chief Judge Walker consolidated the cases for trial. On December 20, the Court denied the Defendants’ motions for summary judgement and Plaintiffs’ motion for partial summary judgment, holding that the remaining claims should be decided on the basis of a complete factual record.

Following bench trial, on March 31, 2022 the Court granted a permanent injunction for the plaintiffs and declared that SB 90 violated the rights of the plaintiffs. Chief Judge Walker found that the law made voting harder and unduly burdened voters with disabilities. The order declared that the challenged provision violated the First Amendment. 

Defendants appealed this decision on April 11, 2022. A week later, the Eleventh Circuit granted Defendant Secretary of State’s unopposed motion to consolidate the appeals. 

On April 27, 2023, Chief Judge William Pryor wrote the opinion of the Court. In relevant part, the Court reversed the judgment that the challenged provisions in SB 90 violated the Voting Rights Act. On September 21, the Eleventh Circuit denied Plaintiffs’ motion to rehear the case en banc. 

Pursuant to the Eleventh Circuit decision, Chief Judge Walker vacated the District Court’s March 2022 decision. In this Order, the Court once again dismissed the Voting Rights Act claim for lack of standing and dismissed the other claims as moot because the provision was repealed. 

Several months later, on February 22, 2024, Plaintiffs in this case renewed their motion for entitlement to attorneys’ fees, which was subsequently granted in part and denied in part in on February 5, 2025. The Court determined that Plaintiff was entitled to $232,412.78 in attorneys’ fees.

Defendants appealed this order on attorneys’ fees on March 4, 2025 to the Eleventh Circuit. On the same day, Defendant Secretary of State moved to consolidate the appeal with similar appeals in the other consolidated cases.

This case is ongoing as of March 12, 2025.

This case challenged Executive Order No. 14343, issued by President Donald Trump on August 28, 2025. The order is a follow-up to Executive Order 14251, which excluded numerous agencies and subdivisions (designated as having primary national security functions) from the collective-bargaining protections of the Federal and Foreign Service Labor-Management Relations Statutes (collectively, the FSLMRS). Executive Order 14343 expanded these exclusions to six additional agencies, including the United States Agency for Global Media (USAGM), removing them from labor coverage under the FSLMRS. In connection with EO 14343, on August 29, 2025, USAGM notified the Plaintiffs that it was terminating its collective bargaining agreements (CBAs) with them. Similar cases challenging Executive Orders 14251 and 14343 are collected here. 

On September 19, 2025, American Federation of State, County & Municipal Employees, AFL-CIO (“AFSCME”) and American Federation of Government Employees, AFL-CIO (“AFGE”), along with their affiliated local unions, filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against President Donald Trump, the USAGM, and Special Advisor to USAGM Kari Lake. Plaintiffs, the designated collective bargaining representatives for USAGM employees, alleged that defendants’ actions violated the First and Fifth Amendments and were ultra vires. Plaintiffs sought a declaratory judgment, injunctive relief, and attorney’s fees. The case was assigned to Judge Paul L. Friedman. 

In their complaint, Plaintiffs asserted that EO 14343 (i) was issued in retaliation for their protected speech and petitioning activities, in violation of the First Amendment, and (ii) exceeded the President’s authority under 5 U.S.C. § 7103(b). They further asserted that the USAGM’s termination of the CBAs pursuant to EO 14343 (i) constituted similar First Amendment retaliation, and (ii) violated the Fifth Amendment by depriving them of constitutionally protected property interests and abrogating property rights in those agreements without due process. 

Also on September 19, 2025, Plaintiffs filed a motion for a preliminary injunction seeking to enjoin Defendants from enforcing or otherwise carrying out Sections 1 and 2 of Executive Order 14343 as they pertain to Plaintiffs’ bargaining units at USAGM and all employees within those units. Defendants were directed to file their motion in opposition by October 31, 2025. 

On the same day, Plaintiffs filed a notice of related cases, asserting that their case involved a common issue of fact and arose from the same event or transaction as nine other cases: Widakuswara v. Lake, 1:25-cv-01015 (D.D.C.); NTEU v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-00935 (D.D.C.); AFGE v. Trump, No. 3:25-cv-03070 (N.D. Cal.); Am. Foreign Serv. Ass’n v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-01030 (D.D.C.); Fed. Educ. Ass’n v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-01362 (D.D.C.); AFL-CIO v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-02445 (D.D.C.); Nat’l Ass’n of Agriculture Employees v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-02657 (D.D.C.); Nat’l Weather Serv. Emps Ass’n v. Trump, No. 1:25-CV-02947 (D.D.C.); and NTEU v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-02990 (D.D.C.). 

On October 7, 2025, the court denied the defendants' objection to the notice of relatedness. Citing its recent decision in Am. Fed’n of Labor-Congress of Indus. Orgs. v. Trump, No. 25-02445 (WL 2301989), the court found that all cases before it challenging Executive Orders 14251 and 14343 are related, as they (1) raise common factual issues and (2) arise from the same underlying event or transaction. The court concluded that the present case is related to each of the cases listed in the plaintiffs’ notice of related cases, except that Widakuswara v. Lake, 1:25-cv-01015 (D.D.C.) and AFGE v. Trump (3:25-cv-03070 N.D. Cal.) were not included in the court's order.

The court granted Plaintiffs’ motion for a preliminary injunction on November 18, 2025, finding that Section 2 of EO 14343 was unlawful as applied to Plaintiffs’ bargaining units. The court enjoined all defendants, except President Trump, from implementing Section 2 with respect to those units.

On December 5, 2025, the parties submitted a joint status report to the Court informing the Court of appeals pending in NTEU v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-00935-PLF (D.D.C); AFSA v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-01030-PLF (D.D.C.); and FEA v. Trump, No. 1:25-cv-1362-PLF (D.D.C.). The parties reported "awaiting a decision on the pending appeals would provide guidance to the parties for motions for summary judgment in this case and could provide guidance on the appropriate scope of potential discovery, if any." Consequently, the parties asked the Court to hold off on requiring them to create a briefing schedule until those appeals are adjudicated. The Court agreed with the parties' reasoning and ordered a stay on this case until resolution of the appeals.

On January 8, 2026, while the district court case was still stayed, Defendants filed an appeal to the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit over the District Court's order granting Plaintiffs' preliminary injunction. The D.C. Court of Appeals set a briefing schedule requiring the parties to file by late February.

This case is ongoing.

On April 10, 2013, the U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights (OCR) received a complaint filed by a university student, which alleged that the Wittenberg University discriminated against her on the basis of sex. Specifically, the complaint alleged that the university failed to respond appropriately, including by conducting a timely investigation, when the student was sexually assaulted by a male student athlete, in violation of Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972.

OCR determined that it had jurisdiction and that the complaint was filed timely, and opened an investigation. They investigated two issues: (1) whether the university interfered with or limited the ability of a student to participate in or benefit from the services, activities, or privileges provided by the university by effectively causing, encouraging, accepting, tolerating, or failing to correct a sexually hostile environment of which it had actual or constructive notice; and (2) whether the university failed to adopt grievance procedures that incorporate appropriate due process standards and that provide for the prompt and equitable resolution of student complaints under Title IX. OCR combined this complaint (15-31-2141) with a complaint from 2011 (15-11-2015) for the investigation, since the allegations in both complaints were substantially similar.

OCR concluded its investigation, summarized in a Letter of Findings dated March 24, 2017. The investigation found that the university violated Title IX. The university failed to indicate that inquiries could be referred to the university's Title IX coordinator or OCR in the university's notification of nondiscrimination. The university also failed to provide a Title IX coordinator during the 2010-2011 school year, and failed to provide notice of its Title IX coordinator from 2010 to March 2017. Additionally, the university failed to comply with Title IX because its grievance procedures only applied to sexual harassment and sexual assault, and not all forms of sex discrimination. The grievance policies also contained conflicting information as to whether they applied to third parties, and expressly allowed for termination of a Title IX proceeding if a party initiated a civil, criminal, or agency proceeding. The university also permitted mediation in matters regarding sexual assault, in violation of Title IX, and failed to provide equitable responses to complaints of sexual assault filed by the two students.

The university entered into a voluntary Resolution Agreement on March 7, 2017. The university was required to adopt and publish an updated Notice of Nondiscrimination, revise its Title IX policies and grievance procedures and submit them for OCR review, and to publish a policy addressing communication between the university's Title IX coordinator and the university's police department. The university also agreed to provide Title IX training to relevant personnel. The Resolution Agreement required the university to issue letters to both student complainants, explaining the steps the university had taken over the past several years to improve its response to sexual assault allegations. The university was also required to reimburse both students or their families for the cost of counseling up to $2000 each that took place after the date of the incident mentioned in the complaints and up to a year following the Resolution Agreement.

The university agreed that OCR monitoring would continue until OCR determined that the university had fulfilled the terms of the agreement and was in compliance with Title IX and its implementing regulation.

On July 9, 2002, the U.S. Department of Justice's Civil Rights Division ("DOJ") sent its "findings letter" to Iowa's governor, advising him of the results of the November 1999, and April-May 2001, DOJ investigations of conditions and practices at two state-operated centers for developmentally and mentally disabled persons: the Woodward State Hospital-School (subsequently renamed Woodward Resource Center) ("Woodward") in Woodward, Iowa, and the Glenwood State Hospital-School (subsequently renamed Glenwood Resource Center) ("Glenwood"), in Glenwood, Iowa. The investigations occurred under the authority of the Civil Rights of Institutionalized Persons Act ("CRIPA"), 42 U.S.C. § 1997. DOJ and expert consultants visited the facilities twice, reviewed a wide array of documents there, and conducted interviews with personnel and residents. The letter commended Woodward and Glenwood staff for providing a high level of cooperation during the investigation, as well as the dedication many showed for resident well-being. Nevertheless, the investigation found deficiencies in resident care at both facilities, in that certain conditions and services at one or both substantially departed from generally accepted standards of care. Constitutional and federal statutory rights of residents at the facilities were violated in several respects, according to the DOJ.

DOJ concluded that deficiencies existed in conditions of resident care and treatment (1) at Woodward (and, to a lesser degree at Glenwood), regarding the use of restraints and restrictive procedures (e.g., over-use of restraints; use of electronic tracking devices at Woodward for staff convenience rather than resident care); (2) at Glenwood, regarding the substandard general medical care provided (e.g., lack of a medical director, a peer review quality assurance tool, a pharmacy and therapeutics committee, or policies and protocols to ensure consistent provision of care; insufficient provision of neurological resources) and, (3) at both centers, in their provision of deficient (a) psychiatric and psychological care (e.g., inadequate psychiatric and psychological assessments and diagnoses; unjustified use of psychotropic medications; poor monitoring of treatment outcomes; defective or non-existent pharmacy and therapeutics oversight; failure to obtain informed consent; inappropriate behavior plan development; non-integrated pharmacological and behavior plans), and (b) community placement programs (neither facility had established protocols ensuring adequate assessments to determine the most integrated setting appropriate for individuals' wants and needs). Nutrition management, physical and occupational therapy, and protection from harm deficiencies were described in the letter, depending on the facility, as ranging from non-existent to notable, with Woodward also described as lacking in appropriate habilitation services. Erroneous and incomplete record keeping plagued both facilities, according to the DOJ. The letter provided details of deficiencies for all of these categories.

Minimally-acceptable remedial measures for each of these categories were outlined in the letter, which concluded by inviting continued further collaboration in implementing the remediation. The letter provided notice that, absent a resolution of the federal concerns, the DOJ would file a CRIPA lawsuit to compel correction of the identified deficiencies at Woodward and Glenwood.

On November 18, 2004, in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Iowa, DOJ simultaneously filed a CRIPA complaint against Iowa and tendered a settlement agreement between the parties, the latter referencing an attached plan obligating the state to implement remedial measures (believed to be those set out in the findings letter; we do not have a copy of the "plan," so we infer this). The lawsuit, seeking declaratory and injunctive relief, set out that the state's practices at Woodward and Glenwood violated their patients' Fourteenth Amendment due process rights and their rights under the Americans with Disabilities Act ("ADA"), 42 U.S.C §§ 12101 et seq., and the ADA's implementing regulations, 28 C.F.R. Part 35.

The settlement obligated the state to ensure, and to periodically report upon its progress in ensuring, improvements that would bring the facilities up to generally accepted professional standards of care. The settlement also allowed for the United States to conduct regular compliance reviews, with facility inspections and interviews of staff and residents, and to fully access and review relevant documents.

The Court (Judge Ronald E. Longstaff) approved the settlement on November 24, 2004. On December 9, 2004, he issued an unpublished order which administratively closed the case, but retained jurisdiction to enforce his order of November 24th.

In April 2009, the DOJ filed a notice to update the court on the monitoring activities and changes at the two facilities, and extending the monitoring period another year. By May 13, 2010, the defendants filed a notice with the court that the consent decrees for both facilities had expired. The case is now closed.

On March 2, 2007, a resident alien and two non-profits filed a class action suit in the U.S. District Court for the District of Massachusetts, challenging the mandatory detention provision of Section 236(c) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The plaintiff also filed a petition for writ of habeas corpus pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 2241, requesting his immediate release from detention.  

On July 12, 2007 district court held a hearing on the writ of habeas corpus, however, before ruling on the issue the plaintiff was released from custody. The court then dismissed the remainder of the case.

On May 18, 2014, inmates at the Clackamas County Jail sued the county and the sheriff over their strip search policy in the U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon. The plaintiffs brought this claim under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, alleging violations of the Fourth, Eighth, and Fourteenth Amendments for strip and visual cavity searches conducted on pre-trial detainees and convicted inmates at the CCJ beginning in May 2012. The court granted class certification in 2018, but upon a motion by the defendants to decertify the class, the court decertified the class in 2019. The plaintiffs appealed this decision to the Ninth Circuit, but the court dismissed the appeal for lack of jurisdiction. The defendants filed a motion for summary judgment on all claims in October 2019. In May 2020, the court granted the motion for summary judgment and dismissed the case with prejudice. The plaintiffs have appealed the decision to the Ninth Circuit. The appeal is ongoing.

On April 15, 2008, American Civil Liberties Union filed a complaint in federal court for the 
Southern District of New York under the Freedom of Information Act seeking immediate processing and release of records plaintiffs requested from defendant Federal Bureau ofInvestigation. Both parties agreed to settle after FOIA request was completed.

This class action, filed in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York on December 28, 2006, successfully challenged the Social Security Administration's policy of suspending or denying benefits to persons with outstanding warrants for alleged violations of probation or parole. The plaintiff class ultimately won an estimated $1 billion in back-benefits.

Jenner & Block, a law firm, sued the Trump administration over an executive order that punished the firm for the cases it handled and the clients it represented. The order cut off government contracts, took away security clearances, limited access to federal buildings and jobs, and ordered an investigation of the firm. Jenner argued this was retaliation and violated the Constitution.

On March 28, 2025, a federal judge stopped the order, saying Jenner was likely to win because the government cannot punish a law firm for its speech or legal work. On May 23, 2025, the judge permanently blocked the order, ruling it was unconstitutional and not justified by national security or anti-discrimination reasons.

The government appealed the decision to a higher court. Similar lawsuits were filed by other major law firms.

Four African American female DC firefighters brought suit against DC. Plaintiffs alleged discriminatory pay, disciplinary, and overtime assignment practices at DC Fire and EMS in violation of Title VII.

In 2012, deaf individual sued the City and County of Denver under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, alleging that the defendants discriminated against the plaintiff by not providing him with the required auxiliary aids and services necessary to ensure effective communication. Defendants made an offer of judgment in the amount of $15,000, which was accepted by the plaintiff.

This is a putative class action suit brought by and on behalf of children with disabilities who alleged that they did not get their educational needs met by their schools due to online learning during the COVID-19 pandemic. On June 30, 2021, the plaintiffs filed this lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Michigan. The plaintiffs sued the Michigan Department of Education, Ann Arbor Public Schools, and Washtenaw Intermediate School District under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (“IDEA”), Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act (“ADA”), the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations (“RICO”) Act of 1970, and state law and regulations.  The court denied plaintiffs’ motion for a temporary restraining order on February 3, 2022.  On July 22, 2022, the district court dismissed the case for lack of subject matter jurisdiction because the plaintiffs lacked standing to pursue injunctive or declaratory relief on all of their remaining claims. Specifically, the plaintiffs alleged only past harm, rather than continuing harm, as to their IDEA, ADA, and Michigan state law claims. The court dismissed their RICO claim because the plaintiffs had failed to establish causation and redressability. On August 17, 2022, the plaintiffs appealed the decision to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit. On May 10, 2023, the Sixth Circuit affirmed the dismissal due to mootness and lack of standing. The Sixth Circuit denied rehearing en banc on June 12, 2023 and affirmed its previous holding. The plaintiffs asked the U.S. Supreme Court to hear the case, but the Court declined to do so on February 20, 2024. The case is now closed.

In January 2021, four teenagers, representing all minors aged 14 to 17 with mental impairments under the legal custody of New Hampshire's Division for Children, Youth and Families (DCYF), filed this putative class-action lawsuit in the United States District Court for the District of New Hampshire. Plaintiffs alleged that DCYF was systematically and unnecessarily institutionalizing older foster youth rather than placing them in homelike settings within or close to their communities, violating their constitutional and statutory rights. In September 2021, the court dismissed the plaintiffs' constitutional claims. On August 21, 2023, the plaintiffs filed a motion for class certification, which was granted on September 18, 2024. A bench trial is scheduled for February 2026.

In 2008, five arrestees filed this class action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey, against Ocean County Correctional Facility, challenging its blanket strip search policy. The court certified the class in 2009. The court granted Defendant’s motion to dismiss, dismissing Plaintiff’s US Constitution claim and state law claims, as the court declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over plaintiffs’ state law claims. The case was closed.

New to the Clearinghouse:  A putative class action lawsuit challenges as racially discriminatory a July 2020 prison raid and related security threat group classifications at the Correctional Training Center.

Yet another case suing @ICEgov and @DHSgov following the July 6 directive that would all force international students to take in-person classes or face deportation. This time it's 7 private plaintiffs repped by @PublicCounsel and @SidleyLaw
Department of Justice appeals lawsuit by asylum seekers seeks to stop ICE from enforcing stricter credible fear criteria to the D.C. Circuit Court.
Ninth Circuit strikes down staff vaccine mandate for California prison officials in one of the country's longest-lasting prison litigation cases.

New in the Clearinghouse: @Uber reached a multi-million dollar settlement with the DOJ @CivilRights, requiring the company to compensate over 65,000 users who were charged wait-time fees as a result of their disability. Check out the documents.

In 2018, kids in the Kansas foster care system filed suit against the Governor and multiple state Departments. A settlement was reached in July 2020, which requires the state to take systemic action to end practices harming kids' health.
Michigan resident alleged he was repeatedly subjected to additional screenings at airports, but the Sixth Circuit ruled that his constitutional right to travel was not impinged.
U.S. district court suspended in-person signature requirement for candidates in Rhode Island for the 2020 election due to COVID-19.

Discovery continues in T.S. v. 20th Century Fox Television, a class action lawsuit brought by 2 children previously detained in the Cook Cty Juvenile Temporary Detention Center. They allege that they were harmed by a lockdown during a TV shoot there

Check out this 2011 @ACLU @PPFA @ReproRights @ case challenging NC's  “Woman’s Right to Know Act.” The Plaintiffs prevailed and the Court reasoned that the “one-size-fits-all speech-and-display provision” violated Plaintiffs’ First Amendment rights.
